title: Apartheid South Africa, 1940s-1960s: Making, Resistance and Repression
subject: History

meta.title: Apartheid South Africa, 1940s-1960s: Making, Resistance and Repression
meta.subject: History

sources.title: The National Party is elected to power in 1948
ANC and the early development of apartheid 1948-1950s
Population Registration Act, Act No 30 of 1950
Banishment under the Suppression of Communism Act of 1950
Bantu education and the racist compartmentalizing of education
Congress of the People and the Freedom Charter
The State versus Nelson Mandela and others - the Rivonia Trial

slides.heading: Apartheid South Africa, 1940s-1960s
slides.lead: The making of apartheid as a system, and the two decades of resistance and repression that ran from the 1948 election to the Rivonia Trial of 1963-64.

image.alt: A South African landscape divided by a hard vertical line, one side ordered into blocks, the other left open, symbolising a society engineered by law.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='320' height='62' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='158' width='4' height='180' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='258' cy='46' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><g fill='#7a8b5a'><rect x='176' y='96' width='22' height='22'/><rect x='204' y='84' width='22' height='34'/><rect x='232' y='100' width='22' height='18'/><rect x='260' y='90' width='22' height='28'/></g><path d='M0 118 L60 100 L110 114 L150 96 L156 118 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by framing the unit's analytical question: apartheid was not simply prejudice but a *system* deliberately built through law after 1948. Preview the three threads - the making of the system (legislation), the resistance to it, and the state's escalating repression - and explain that the unit closes with the Rivonia Trial, the moment the state jailed the leadership of the liberation movement and drove it into exile and underground.

slides.heading: Segregation before 1948
slides.lead: Apartheid did not appear from nowhere - it systematised and hardened decades of existing segregation.

blocks.text: By the 1940s South Africa was already deeply segregated. The **1913 Natives Land Act** reserved roughly 87% of the land for the white minority, **pass laws** controlled the movement of black men, and the colour bar limited black workers to unskilled jobs. What changed after 1948 was the *scale, coherence and intent* of segregation.

items: Segregation under earlier governments was extensive but uneven and incomplete.
Rapid wartime urbanisation of black workers alarmed white authorities.
Afrikaner nationalism and pseudo-scientific 'ideas of race' gave apartheid its ideology.

slides.notes: Stress the CAPS concept of continuity and change. Learners should be able to argue both that apartheid built on older segregation AND that 1948 marked a qualitative break - a comprehensive blueprint to engineer an entire society by race. Link to the Grade 11 topic 'Ideas of race in the late 19th and 20th centuries' to explain where apartheid's ideology came from.

slides.heading: The 1948 election and the National Party
slides.lead: The National Party won power on the explicit promise of apartheid.

blocks.text: The **National Party (NP)** under **D.F. Malan**, in alliance with the Afrikaner Party, defeated Jan Smuts's United Party. Its 1948 election blueprint (the **Sauer Report**) called for total racial separation. Crucially, the NP won a majority of *seats* while winning fewer total *votes* than the United Party, because rural constituencies were weighted more heavily.

items: Only white voters could take part; the black majority had no vote.
'Apartheid' (Afrikaans for 'apartness') became official state policy.
The NP would then convert its ideology into a chain of laws from 1949 onward.

image.alt: A ballot box receiving a folded paper, with a bar chart behind it showing more votes on one side but more seats on the other.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='120' y='40' width='18' height='70' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='144' y='64' width='18' height='46' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='168' y='30' width='18' height='80' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='55' y='78' width='70' height='58' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='68' y='66' width='46' height='14' rx='2' fill='#5c1414'/><rect x='80' y='40' width='24' height='30' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2' transform='rotate(-12 92 55)'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the pivotal event of the unit. Draw out the legitimacy problem for analysis: a government that built a system denying rights to the majority itself came to power on a minority of the white vote, in an election the majority of South Africans could not enter. That gap between claimed authority and actual consent runs through the whole apartheid period.

slides.heading: The architecture of apartheid
slides.lead: Historians distinguish 'petty' apartheid (daily segregation) from 'grand' apartheid (territorial separation into homelands).

blocks.heading: Classify
blocks.text: Fix every person into a legal racial group as the foundation for all other laws.

blocks.heading: Separate
blocks.text: Divide residence, marriage, schooling, amenities and, ultimately, citizenship by race.

blocks.heading: Control
blocks.text: Silence, ban, detain and prosecute anyone who challenged the system.

slides.notes: Give learners this three-verb framework (classify - separate - control) so the many Acts that follow are understood as parts of a single design rather than a list to memorise. Introduce the petty/grand apartheid distinction now; you will return to grand apartheid at the Bantustans slide. Ask learners to sort each law that follows into one of the three functions.

slides.heading: The foundational laws
slides.lead: Between 1949 and 1950 the state built the legal skeleton of apartheid.

items: **Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act, No. 55 of 1949** - banned marriage across racial lines.
**Immorality Amendment Act, No. 21 of 1950** - criminalised sexual relations across racial lines.
**Population Registration Act, No. 30 of 1950** - classified every person as White, Coloured or Native (Indian/Asian added as a distinct group), the master key for every other law.
**Group Areas Act, No. 41 of 1950** - divided towns into racial zones, enabling mass forced removals such as Sophiatown and District Six.

image.alt: A reference-book icon labelled with stacked law pages and a state stamp, over a city map split into coloured zones.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='24' y='24' width='84' height='58' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='116' y='24' width='80' height='58' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='24' y='90' width='84' height='46' fill='#a8bcae'/><rect x='116' y='90' width='80' height='46' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='110' y='20' width='4' height='120' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='20' y='84' width='180' height='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='158' cy='113' r='15' fill='none' stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='150' y='109' width='16' height='8' fill='#5c1414'/></svg>

slides.notes: At FET level, push beyond naming Acts to explaining how they interlocked. Classification (Act 30) had to come first because it decided who each other law applied to. Note the intimate reach of the state - Acts 55 and 21 policed marriage and sex - and the material dispossession of Group Areas. [SME] Confirm the Act numbers and years against the SAHO apartheid-legislation article before production; the 1949-1950 dates are well established but numbering is worth a check.

slides.heading: Everyday apartheid and Bantu Education
slides.lead: Further laws reached into public space, movement and the minds of children.

items: **Reservation of Separate Amenities Act, No. 49 of 1953** - segregated public facilities and allowed them to be openly unequal ('separate but not equal').
**Natives (Abolition of Passes and Co-ordination of Documents) Act, 1952** - despite its name, tightened and extended the pass system (the 'reference book').
**Bantu Education Act, No. 47 of 1953** - took black schooling from the churches and, in Verwoerd's own words, aimed to prepare black children only for labour.

blocks.text: There is no place for [the African] in the European community above the level of certain forms of labour.
blocks.cite: H.F. Verwoerd, Minister of Native Affairs, on Bantu Education, 1953

slides.notes: The Verwoerd quotation makes the intent explicit and is a strong source for analysis - ask learners what it reveals about the *purpose* of apartheid education. Draw the long line forward: anger at Bantu Education fed the 1976 Soweto Uprising, which learners meet in the next Grade 12 topic. [SME] The Verwoerd quotation is widely cited but often paraphrased; verify exact wording and citation before production.

slides.heading: Bantustans: engineering citizenship
slides.lead: The state tried to redefine black South Africans as citizens of separate 'homelands' rather than of South Africa.

blocks.text: **Grand apartheid** aimed to divide the country territorially. The **Bantu Authorities Act, No. 68 of 1951** set up tribal authorities in the reserves, and the **Promotion of Bantu Self-Government Act, No. 46 of 1959** - driven by Verwoerd - grouped Africans into separate ethnic 'national units' that would supposedly become self-governing homelands.

items: The long-term goal was to deny Africans citizenship of a common South Africa.
The homelands held a fraction of the land and lacked economic viability.
Chiefs were co-opted, splitting communities and undermining resistance.

image.alt: A map of a country with several small fragmented patches shaded differently and pushed to the margins.

slides.notes: This is the analytically hardest slide - make sure learners grasp that grand apartheid was about *citizenship*, not just where people lived. By assigning Africans to homelands, the state intended to strip them of any claim to political rights in the 87% of South Africa reserved for whites. This becomes the basis for the 'independent' Bantustans of the 1970s. [SME] The 87%/13% land split figures are standard but confirm phrasing.

slides.heading: Laws to silence opposition
slides.lead: Alongside separation, the state built legal machinery to crush dissent.

blocks.text: The **Suppression of Communism Act, No. 44 of 1950** defined 'communism' so broadly that it could be used against almost any opponent - banning people, restricting their movement and silencing them without trial. Through the early 1960s this was reinforced by the **Sabotage Act (General Law Amendment Act, 1962)** and detention-without-trial laws (the 90-day detention clause of 1963).

items: 'Banning' and 'listing' silenced leaders without a court hearing.
Vague definitions let the state target unionists, liberals and church figures.
Detention without trial removed basic legal protection for activists.

image.alt: A speech bubble struck through by a heavy red line, symbolising silenced voices.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M40 40 h120 a12 12 0 0 1 12 12 v44 a12 12 0 0 1 -12 12 h-58 l-24 22 v-22 h-38 a12 12 0 0 1 -12 -12 v-44 a12 12 0 0 1 12 -12 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/><line x1='30' y1='118' x2='176' y2='34' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='10' stroke-linecap='round'/></svg>

slides.notes: Frame this around the rule of law: apartheid's security laws punished people without fair trial and made vague, sweeping definitions the basis for prosecution. The 1962 and 1963 laws are slightly later than the founding legislation but belong here because they show the state escalating its legal weapons as resistance grew - setting the stage for Rivonia. [SME] Confirm the '90-day' clause is dated 1963 (General Law Amendment Act) before production.

slides.heading: Defiance Campaign and the Freedom Charter
slides.lead: The 1950s saw the largest organised, multi-racial, non-violent resistance yet seen in South Africa.

blocks.text: The **Defiance Campaign of 1952**, led by the **ANC** and the **South African Indian Congress**, mobilised thousands of volunteers to break apartheid laws deliberately and court arrest. In 1955 the **Congress of the People** at Kliptown adopted the **Freedom Charter**, whose opening line declared that 'South Africa belongs to all who live in it, black and white.'

items: The Defiance Campaign drove a sharp rise in ANC membership.
The Congress Alliance united the ANC with Indian, Coloured and white democrats.
The Freedom Charter later shaped the values of South Africa's 1996 Constitution.

image.alt: A line of marching figures raising their hands, a banner above them.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='40' y='24' width='160' height='18' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='45' cy='72' r='11'/><rect x='39' y='84' width='13' height='40'/><circle cx='95' cy='68' r='11'/><rect x='89' y='80' width='13' height='44'/><circle cx='145' cy='72' r='11'/><rect x='139' y='84' width='13' height='40'/><circle cx='195' cy='66' r='11'/><rect x='189' y='78' width='13' height='46'/></g><rect x='36' y='134' width='170' height='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide compresses resistance the Grade 9 curriculum treats in detail; at Grade 11, focus on strategy and significance rather than narrative. Emphasise the choice of *non-violent mass action* and the *Congress Alliance* model of multi-racial cooperation. Note the deliberate contrast the learners should draw: a movement offering an inclusive vision versus a state offering exclusion by law.

slides.heading: Treason Trial and Sharpeville
slides.lead: The state answered resistance with prosecution and, at Sharpeville, with lethal force.

blocks.text: In December 1956 the state arrested **156 activists** and charged them with high treason, using the Freedom Charter as evidence of a supposed violent plot; the **Treason Trial** dragged on until **1961**, when all the accused were acquitted. Then on **21 March 1960** police opened fire on an anti-pass protest at **Sharpeville**, killing 69 people, many shot in the back.

items: The Sharpeville protest was called by the **Pan Africanist Congress (PAC)** under Robert Sobukwe.
The state declared a State of Emergency and banned the ANC and PAC in April 1960.
Sharpeville turned world opinion sharply against apartheid.

image.alt: A scale of justice on one side and a stark warning burst on the other, over a dark horizon line.

slides.notes: Two events, one theme: the state's turn from legal harassment to open violence. Highlight the irony of the Treason Trial - years of prosecution ending in total acquittal, exposing the state's case as political. Then Sharpeville marks a rupture: the banning of the ANC and PAC closed off legal, non-violent politics and pushed the movements underground. [SME] The Sharpeville death toll of 69 is the standard figure; confirm before production.

slides.heading: The turn to armed struggle
slides.lead: With peaceful protest banned, the liberation movements took up arms.

blocks.text: After the bannings of 1960, the ANC and its allies concluded that non-violent protest alone could no longer work against a state willing to shoot and jail. In 1961 they formed **Umkhonto we Sizwe (MK, 'Spear of the Nation')**, which launched a sabotage campaign on **16 December 1961**, targeting installations while trying to avoid loss of life. The PAC formed its own armed wing, **Poqo**.

items: MK initially chose sabotage over guerrilla war to limit casualties.
The decision marked a historic shift after decades of non-violent struggle.
The move gave the state a pretext for its harshest security laws yet.

slides.notes: This is a sensitive point requiring balance. Explain the movement's reasoning - the closing of all legal avenues after 1960 - without either glorifying or condemning violence. Note MK's stated policy of sabotage aimed at property rather than people at this stage. This decision leads directly to Rivonia: it is the underground MK leadership the state captures next. [SME] Confirm the 16 December 1961 launch date.

slides.eyebrow: The state strikes at the leadership - 1963-64
slides.heading: The Rivonia Trial
slides.lead: A police raid on Liliesleaf Farm led to the trial that jailed the liberation movement's leaders.

blocks.text: On **11 July 1963** police raided **Liliesleaf Farm in Rivonia**, near Johannesburg, the secret headquarters of the MK high command, seizing documents and arresting much of the leadership. In the trial that followed (October 1963 - June 1964), the accused were charged with sabotage. On **12 June 1964** eight men - including **Nelson Mandela, Walter Sisulu and Govan Mbeki** - were sentenced to **life imprisonment** rather than death.
I have cherished the ideal of a democratic and free society... It is an ideal for which I am prepared to die.
blocks.cite: Nelson Mandela, statement from the dock, Rivonia Trial, 20 April 1964

slides.notes: Mandela's speech from the dock is a set-piece source for FET analysis - discuss how he turned a trial meant to condemn the movement into a platform for its cause. The life sentences (rather than execution) reflected both legal caution and international pressure. Stress the outcome for the unit: by 1964 the internal leadership was jailed or exiled, and the struggle entered its long underground and international phase. [SME] Verify the full list of the eight sentenced and confirm the 12 June 1964 sentencing date and 20 April 1964 speech date before production.

slides.heading: Making, resistance and repression, 1948-1964

blocks.alt: A timeline from 1948 to 1964 tracking apartheid legislation alongside resistance and state repression.

events.label: National Party wins power; apartheid becomes state policy
Population Registration, Group Areas and Suppression of Communism Acts
Bantu Education and Separate Amenities Acts
Sharpeville massacre; ANC and PAC banned

slides.notes: Use this to consolidate the unit's two interwoven strands before assessment. Ask learners to trace how each burst of resistance provoked a harsher state response, and how state repression in turn reshaped resistance - most sharply the 1960 bannings driving the 1961 turn to arms. This cause-and-effect chain is exactly what CAPS extended-writing questions reward.

slides.heading: What we learned
slides.lead: By 1964 apartheid was fully entrenched in law and its main opponents were jailed or exiled - yet the ideals of the Freedom Charter survived.

blocks.text: In under two decades the National Party engineered a comprehensive system of racial domination through law, met rising resistance with escalating repression, and by the Rivonia Trial had driven the liberation movement underground and abroad. The struggle was contained, not defeated - the vision set out in the 1950s would shape a democratic South Africa four decades later.

blocks.question: Explain how the Population Registration Act of 1950 formed the foundation for the rest of apartheid legislation.
Distinguish between 'petty' and 'grand' apartheid, using one law as an example of each.
How far do you agree that the bannings of 1960 made the turn to armed struggle inevitable? Support your answer with evidence.

slides.notes: Close the loop between the unit's threads: legislation, resistance and repression converged at Rivonia. Use the three questions as written assessment - the mark allocations rise from source-recall (6) through conceptual distinction (8) to an FET-level argument question (10) that expects a line of argument and evidence. Remind learners this sets up the Grade 12 topic on civil resistance in the 1970s-1980s and the coming of democracy.
